subject: Chemistry
title: Iridescence: Color Without Pigment
description: Learn Iridescence: Color Without Pigment in one minute.

hook.text: Soap bubbles and peacock feathers aren't dyed—their colors come from light bouncing off microscopic layers.

buildup.text: Thin films or nanostructures split light into waves. Some waves reinforce each other; others cancel out.

discovery.text: This is called interference. The color you see depends on the angle of view and the thickness of the layers.

twist.text: Morpho butterflies are brown. Their brilliant blue comes from nanoscale ridges on wing scales, not any pigment.

climax.text: Scientists copy this for color-shifting paint, anti-counterfeit bills, and displays that need no power to show color.

punchline.text: No dye, just structure. Nanoscale architecture creates color from geometry.

quiz.question: How do Morpho butterflies produce their brilliant blue color?
options: Nanoscale ridges on wing scales create light interference, not pigment
Blue pigment molecules are concentrated in their wings
They absorb UV light and re-emit it as blue
